cves.description:
evernote-mcp-server openBrowser Command Injection Privilege Escalation Vulnerability. This vulnerability allows local attackers to escalate privileges on affected installations of evernote-mcp-server. An attacker must first obtain the ability to execute low-privileged code on the target system in order to exploit this vulnerability.

The specific flaw exists within the openBrowser function. The issue results from the lack of proper validation of a user-supplied string before using it to execute
cves.how_introduced: 1. Blamed commit e08547 introduces auth.js and the child_process.exec dependency. 2. It implements the openBrowser function using shell execution: exec(`${command} "${url}"`). 3. This pattern allows an attacker to break out of the URL string and execute arbitrary system commands via the shell.
cves.root_cause: The openBrowser function failed to properly validate or sanitize user-supplied input, allowing an attacker to inject arbitrary commands into the system shell execution.
cves.vulnerable_pattern: Passing unvalidated user input directly into a system command execution sink (e.g., child_process.exec or similar) within the openBrowser function.

bug_commits.author: brentmid
bug_commits.message: Add comprehensive OAuth 1.0a authentication and testing suite

screening_verification.reasoning: The blamed commit introduced the entire initial implementation of `auth.js`, including the use of `child_process.exec` to open the system browser. This specific implementation used template literals to concatenate a user-controlled URL directly into a shell command, which is the root cause of the command injection vulnerability fixed in the subsequent commit.
screening_verification.vuln_description: The openBrowser function failed to properly validate or sanitize user-supplied input, allowing an attacker to inject arbitrary commands into the system shell execution.
screening_verification.vulnerable_pattern: Passing unvalidated user input directly into a system command execution sink (e.g., child_process.exec or similar) within the openBrowser function.
screening_verification.causal_chain: 1. Blamed commit e08547 introduces auth.js and the child_process.exec dependency. 2. It implements the openBrowser function using shell execution: exec(`${command} "${url}"`). 3. This pattern allows an attacker to break out of the URL string and execute arbitrary system commands via the shell.

agent_verdicts.reasoning: This commit created auth.js and introduced the vulnerable implementation outright: it added `const { exec } = require('child_process');`, `function openBrowser(url)`, and `exec(`${command} "${url}"`, ...)`. It also introduced the data flow into that sink by building `authUrl` from `requestToken` and passing it to `openBrowser(authUrl)`. Pickaxe for the exact `exec(`${command} "${url}"`...)` string and for `function openBrowser(url)` points back to this commit, and blame on the fix-parent attributes the key lines to e08547bc.

cves.description: A vulnerability has been found in mruby up to 3.4.0. This vulnerability affects the function sort_cmp of the file src/array.c. Such manipulation leads to use after free. An attack has to be approached locally. The exploit has been disclosed to the public and may be used. The name of the patch is eb398971bfb43c38db3e04528b68ac9a7ce509bc. It is advisable to implement a patch to correct this issue.
cves.how_introduced: The blamed commit introduced a comparison mechanism in `sort_cmp` that captures a pointer `p` (RARRAY_PTR(ary)) to detect array reallocations. However, this check was insufficient to detect all forms of reallocations or modifications (specifically those changing length or buffer content without necessarily changing the head pointer), allowing the heapify or insertion sort logic to continue using stale pointers into a reallocated buffer, leading to use-after-free.
cves.root_cause: The Array#sort! function in mruby suffered from a use-after-realloc vulnerability within sort_cmp, where the array buffer could be reallocated during the sorting process, causing existing pointers to become invalid.
cves.vulnerable_pattern: Accessing array elements or pointers that may have been invalidated by a realloc call triggered within the sort comparison function.

bug_commits.author: Yukihiro "Matz" Matsumoto
bug_commits.message: array: implement hybrid sorting with algorithm selection

screening_verification.reasoning: The blamed commit introduced the `sort_cmp` function signature and logic that relied on the pointer `p` to detect array modifications. By storing the pointer `p` as the base address of the array at the start of the sort and only checking `RARRAY_PTR(ary) != p` later, it failed to account for scenarios where `realloc` changes the buffer size without changing the base pointer, or where the length of the array changes in a way that invalidates the previously captured pointer `p` before the check occurs.
screening_verification.vuln_description: The Array#sort! function in mruby suffered from a use-after-realloc vulnerability within sort_cmp, where the array buffer could be reallocated during the sorting process, causing existing pointers to become invalid.
screening_verification.vulnerable_pattern: Accessing array elements or pointers that may have been invalidated by a realloc call triggered within the sort comparison function.
screening_verification.causal_chain: The blamed commit introduced a comparison mechanism in `sort_cmp` that captures a pointer `p` (RARRAY_PTR(ary)) to detect array reallocations. However, this check was insufficient to detect all forms of reallocations or modifications (specifically those changing length or buffer content without necessarily changing the head pointer), allowing the heapify or insertion sort logic to continue using stale pointers into a reallocated buffer, leading to use-after-free.

agent_verdicts.reasoning: This commit is the direct origin of the code that eb398 fixes. It rewrote `sort_cmp` to take element values instead of indices and removed the prior `size < a || size < b` length/index validation, leaving only `if (RARRAY_PTR(ary) != p)` as the mutation check. Pickaxe shows the pointer-only check and the value-based `sort_cmp(...)` signature first appear here, which is exactly the vulnerable pattern that allowed in-place shrink/realloc with the same pointer to go undetected.

cves.description: vet MCP Server SSE Transport DNS Rebinding Vulnerability
cves.how_introduced: This earlier commit first added SSE transport support and exposed it from the CLI via `--server-type sse`, serving `server.NewSSEServer(...)` directly with `s.Start(config.SseServerAddr)` and no host/origin restrictions or config for them. The fix adds exactly those missing controls in `guard.go` and `server.go`, and pickaxe on the SSE construction/start strings traces the earliest introduction back to this commit. Since the raw SSE handler already returned wildcard CORS on GET (as shown by the integration expectations around the unchanged underlying handler), the core DNS-rebinding-prone design existed from the first SSE implementation.

bug_commits.author: copilot-swe-agent[bot]
bug_commits.message: Add HTTP HEAD request support to SSE MCP server

screening_verification.reasoning: The blamed commit introduced `sseHandlerWithHeadSupport`, which explicitly set `Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *` and failed to implement any origin or Host header validation for SSE requests. This established the insecure handling pattern that allowed the DNS rebinding vulnerability, which was subsequently mitigated in the fix commit by introducing `originGuard` and `hostGuard` wrappers.
screening_verification.vuln_description: The MCP Server SSE transport lacked validation of the Host header, allowing potential DNS rebinding attacks where a malicious website could interact with local MCP server instances.
screening_verification.vulnerable_pattern: Lack of Host header verification or origin checking in SSE transport connections, allowing unauthorized requests to bypass same-origin protections.
screening_verification.causal_chain: The blamed commit added an HTTP handler that accepted SSE requests without validating the origin or Host header, and specifically included a permissive wildcard CORS policy (Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *), thereby creating the vulnerable surface area.

agent_verdicts.reasoning: This commit introduced `sseHandlerWithHeadSupport` and explicitly set `Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *` for `HEAD /sse`, which the fix later changes to origin-reflection behind `originGuard`. It also rewired SSE serving to `wrappedHandler := sseHandlerWithHeadSupport(s)` on a raw `http.Server` with no host or origin validation, and blame on the pre-fix file shows the fixed HEAD-handler and wrapper lines were authored here. The screening hypothesis is directionally correct, but incomplete: this commit added a new vulnerable HEAD path and preserved the lack of guards, rather than being the first origin of the overall SSE exposure.

cves.description: A flaw has been found in mruby up to 3.4.0. This affects the function mrb_vm_exec of the file src/vm.c of the component JMPNOT-to-JMPIF Optimization. Executing a manipulation can lead to use after free. The attack needs to be launched locally. The exploit has been published and may be used. This patch is called e50f15c1c6e131fa7934355eb02b8173b13df415. It is advisable to implement a patch to correct this issue.
cves.how_introduced: The blamed commit implemented an optimization that assumes the instruction at `fail_pos - 2` is always `OP_JMPNOT`. Without this check, other instruction types (like JMP from undefined variables) could be incorrectly overwritten with `OP_JMPIF`, corrupting the bytecode execution and triggering the use-after-free.
cves.root_cause: The JMPNOT-to-JMPIF bytecode optimization creates bytecode corruption leading to a use-after-free condition in the mruby virtual machine during execution.
cves.vulnerable_pattern: Incorrect bytecode generation logic during JMPNOT-to-JMPIF optimization that produces invalid jump targets or corrupts stack state

bug_commits.author: Yukihiro "Matz" Matsumoto
bug_commits.message: mruby-compiler: optimize JMPNOT+JMP to JMPIF in pattern matching

screening_verification.reasoning: The blamed commit introduced the JMPNOT-to-JMPIF optimization logic which lacked validation to ensure the instruction being replaced was actually an OP_JMPNOT. The fix subsequently added this missing check (`s->iseq[fail_pos - 2] == OP_JMPNOT`), confirming that the original implementation allowed incorrect instructions to be overwritten, leading to the bytecode corruption.
screening_verification.vuln_description: The JMPNOT-to-JMPIF bytecode optimization creates bytecode corruption leading to a use-after-free condition in the mruby virtual machine during execution.
screening_verification.vulnerable_pattern: Incorrect bytecode generation logic during JMPNOT-to-JMPIF optimization that produces invalid jump targets or corrupts stack state
screening_verification.causal_chain: The blamed commit implemented an optimization that assumes the instruction at `fail_pos - 2` is always `OP_JMPNOT`. Without this check, other instruction types (like JMP from undefined variables) could be incorrectly overwritten with `OP_JMPIF`, corrupting the bytecode execution and triggering the use-after-free.

agent_verdicts.reasoning: This commit is the first one that adds the unsafe JMPNOT-to-JMPIF rewrite in the top-level pattern-match failure handler: it introduces the `fail_pos + 2 == s->pc` check and then writes `s->iseq[fail_pos - 2] = OP_JMPIF`. The fix commit e50f15c1 adds the missing guard `s->iseq[fail_pos - 2] == OP_JMPNOT`, showing that this exact rewrite was the vulnerable logic. Pickaxe on `s->iseq[fail_pos - 2] = OP_JMPIF;` and `fail_pos + 2 == s->pc` traces both strings back to 2b72, and no earlier commit performed this bytecode mutation.

cves.description: eBay API MCP Server Affected by Environment Variable Injection
cves.how_introduced: The blamed commit introduced the `updateEnvFile` utility using a flawed regex-based string manipulation approach (`const newLine = ${key}=${value};`), which directly concatenates untrusted input into the environment file configuration, directly enabling the reported injection vulnerability.
cves.root_cause: The application fails to properly sanitize or validate configuration inputs, allowing an attacker to inject malicious environment variables via the OAuth process.
cves.vulnerable_pattern: Unsanitized input being passed directly into environment variable configuration or process execution context during the OAuth flow.

bug_commits.author: Claude
bug_commits.message: feat: Auto-update .env file with refreshed tokens

screening_verification.reasoning: The blamed commit introduced the `updateEnvFile` function, which uses simple string replacement and concatenation to write user-provided tokens (like access and refresh tokens) directly into the `.env` file without proper quoting or escaping. This implementation pattern allows for environment variable injection because an attacker-supplied token containing newlines or extra assignments could modify or append arbitrary entries to the .env file.
screening_verification.vuln_description: The application fails to properly sanitize or validate configuration inputs, allowing an attacker to inject malicious environment variables via the OAuth process.
screening_verification.vulnerable_pattern: Unsanitized input being passed directly into environment variable configuration or process execution context during the OAuth flow.
screening_verification.causal_chain: The blamed commit introduced the `updateEnvFile` utility using a flawed regex-based string manipulation approach (`const newLine = ${key}=${value};`), which directly concatenates untrusted input into the environment file configuration, directly enabling the reported injection vulnerability.

agent_verdicts.reasoning: The fix replaces the entire `updateEnvFile()` helper, and pickaxe shows `function updateEnvFile(`, `updateEnvFile({`, and the original sink `const newLine = `${key}=${value}`` were all first introduced in this commit. Its diff added both the ad-hoc regex/string-replacement implementation and the call sites that persist token values from `setUserTokens()`, `getOrRefreshAppAccessToken()`, and `refreshUserToken()` into `.env`. That means this commit created the vulnerable design: untrusted token values were concatenated directly into `.env` content without safe escaping/serialization, enabling environment-variable injection.

bug_commits.author: George Peden
bug_commits.message: Fix: quote token values in .env to prevent # truncation

screening_verification.reasoning: The blamed commit introduced the vulnerable pattern by explicitly adding double quotes around the `value` in the regex replacement logic (`newLine = `${key}="${value}"``). While intended to improve formatting, this logic did not account for malicious payloads containing double quotes or newlines, which allowed for environment variable injection when writing to the .env file.
screening_verification.vuln_description: The application fails to properly sanitize or validate configuration inputs, allowing an attacker to inject malicious environment variables via the OAuth process.
screening_verification.vulnerable_pattern: Unsanitized input being passed directly into environment variable configuration or process execution context during the OAuth flow.
screening_verification.causal_chain: The commit 8c1989e36ad2 changed the line handling .env updates to wrap values in double quotes without escaping existing quotes or sanitizing the input. This permitted an attacker to inject characters (like `"`) into the OAuth process, which the subsequent vulnerable `updateEnvFile` function then wrote into the .env file, leading to arbitrary configuration injection.

agent_verdicts.reasoning: This commit rewrote the vulnerable serialization line from `const newLine = `${key}=${value}`` to `const newLine = `${key}="${value}"``, and pickaxe shows 8c1989e is the first commit to add that exact quoted form. Although it tried to fix `#` truncation, it still wrote attacker-controlled values into `.env` via raw interpolation, now without escaping embedded quotes/newlines. The fix later removes this exact line and replaces the whole approach with `dotenv.parse` + `dotenv-stringify`, so 8c1989e is also a bug-introducing reimplementation of the same vulnerability.

cves.description: zae-limiter: DynamoDB hot partition throttling enables per-entity Denial of Service
cves.how_introduced: The blamed commit implemented `_is_wcu_exhausted` using a simplified logic check (comparing tokens_milli < 1000) that failed to correctly interpret DynamoDB partition throttling states. This logic error meant that when the system encountered high contention or throttling, it incorrectly triggered shard doubling or retries without proper deduplication or capacity validation, ultimately leading to the reported DoS condition.
cves.root_cause: The application failed to gracefully handle DynamoDB per-partition throttling (hot partitions) and did not properly aggregate capacity across shards, allowing an attacker or heavy load to exhaust resources and trigger a DoS condition per entity.
cves.vulnerable_pattern: Lack of robust handling for DynamoDB throttles (ProvisionedThroughputExceededException/ThrottlingException) during speculative consumption, and logic errors in capacity calculation ('get_resource_capacity') that failed to correctly deduplicate sharded entities, leading to inaccurate capacity accounting.

bug_commits.author: Patrick Sodré
bug_commits.message: ✨ feat(limiter): add shard selection, retry on exhaustion, and shard doubling

screening_verification.reasoning: The blamed commit introduced the `_is_wcu_exhausted` logic and the initial handling of speculative failures related to shard limits, which the vulnerability analysis identifies as flawed. Specifically, this commit added the rudimentary check that failed to account for correct capacity aggregation and proper handling of DynamoDB throttling states, which the fix commit subsequently replaces with more granular `SpeculativeFailureReason` checks.
screening_verification.vuln_description: The application failed to gracefully handle DynamoDB per-partition throttling (hot partitions) and did not properly aggregate capacity across shards, allowing an attacker or heavy load to exhaust resources and trigger a DoS condition per entity.
screening_verification.vulnerable_pattern: Lack of robust handling for DynamoDB throttles (ProvisionedThroughputExceededException/ThrottlingException) during speculative consumption, and logic errors in capacity calculation ('get_resource_capacity') that failed to correctly deduplicate sharded entities, leading to inaccurate capacity accounting.
screening_verification.causal_chain: The blamed commit implemented `_is_wcu_exhausted` using a simplified logic check (comparing tokens_milli < 1000) that failed to correctly interpret DynamoDB partition throttling states. This logic error meant that when the system encountered high contention or throttling, it incorrectly triggered shard doubling or retries without proper deduplication or capacity validation, ultimately leading to the reported DoS condition.

agent_verdicts.reasoning: This commit introduced the shard-doubling and shard-retry logic in `RateLimiter`, including `_is_wcu_exhausted()` and the `old_buckets`-based decision path. The fix replaces that inference with an explicit `SpeculativeFailureReason` from the repository and only retries on `APP_LIMIT_EXHAUSTED`, showing that 9f66's original recovery logic was the flawed behavior. In particular, using raw bucket token state to infer why the conditional write failed made the hot-partition mitigation nondeterministic under mixed app-limit/WCU exhaustion.

bug_commits.author: Patrick Sodré
bug_commits.message: Initial commit: zae-limiter rate limiting library

agent_verdicts.reasoning: Although the blamed `limiter.py` capacity-reporting lines are not themselves security-relevant, the initial commit also introduced the original bucket key scheme in `schema.py`/`repository.py` that stored bucket writes under `PK=ENTITY#{entity_id}`. That design concentrated all writes for a given entity onto one DynamoDB partition, which is the root hot-partition condition later mitigated by per-shard bucket PKs. So this commit is a real origin point for the CVE, even though blame drift surfaced unrelated lines.

bug_commits.author: Patrick Sodré
bug_commits.message: ✨ feat(aggregator): filter wcu, proactive sharding, shard propagation

screening_verification.reasoning: The blamed commit (6bcd260679f3) introduced the initial implementation of `try_proactive_shard` and its associated logic using `wcu_tc_delta` (consumption delta) as the metric for scaling. This logic was flawed because it monitored recent consumption deltas rather than actual token levels, failing to correctly account for sustained load, which directly matches the root cause described in the vulnerability analysis.
screening_verification.vuln_description: The application failed to gracefully handle DynamoDB per-partition throttling (hot partitions) and did not properly aggregate capacity across shards, allowing an attacker or heavy load to exhaust resources and trigger a DoS condition per entity.
screening_verification.vulnerable_pattern: Lack of robust handling for DynamoDB throttles (ProvisionedThroughputExceededException/ThrottlingException) during speculative consumption, and logic errors in capacity calculation ('get_resource_capacity') that failed to correctly deduplicate sharded entities, leading to inaccurate capacity accounting.
screening_verification.causal_chain: The commit implemented a proactive sharding mechanism that incorrectly relied on 'wcu_tc_delta' (recent consumption delta) to trigger scaling, rather than assessing sustained capacity depletion, leading to inadequate resource scaling under high-load scenarios.

agent_verdicts.reasoning: This commit introduced both vulnerable aggregator behaviors that 481ce44 fixes: proactive sharding based on `wcu_tc_delta / capacity` and shard propagation that only `UpdateItem`d `shard_count` on peer shards instead of pre-creating full shard items. The fix directly rewrites both pieces, switching to token-level headroom and creating actual shard items so clients can distribute load. That makes 6bcd a direct bug-introducing commit for the hot-partition mitigation failure.

bug_commits.author: Patrick Sodré
bug_commits.message: ♻️ refactor(schema): implement composite bucket items with ADD-based writes

agent_verdicts.reasoning: This composite-bucket rewrite reimplemented bucket storage around a single entity partition key (`PK=pk_entity(entity_id)`, `SK=sk_bucket(resource)`) for all writes to an entity/resource bucket. That preserved the same hot-partition DoS condition in newly written code, even though the on-disk format changed. Because the later sharding work was specifically introduced to replace this layout, 663b is a genuine reintroduction of the vulnerable design.

cves.description: ZeptoClaw: Email Sender Spoofing to bypass Header-Only From Allowlist Validation
cves.how_introduced: This commit introduced the entire email channel, including `EmailConfig.allowed_senders`, `is_sender_allowed()`, and the `process_unseen()` logic that parses `from` from the inbound email's `From` header (`parsed.from()...address()`), checks that header value against the allowlist, and then uses the same `from` as the inbound sender/chat identity. The later fix `bf004a20` only adds documentation and runtime/config warnings that `allowed_senders` trusts the parsed `From` header, so the flawed trust model was present from the feature's first implementation. Pickaxe on `allowed_senders` and `InboundMessage::new("email", &from, &from, &content)` also traces those vulnerable patterns back to this commit.

cves.description: zeptoclaw has Shell allowlist-blocklist bypass via command/argument injection and file name wildcards
cves.how_introduced: 1. Blamed commit introduces `REGEX_BLOCKED_PATTERNS` using rigid space-sensitive regexes like `python[23]?\s+-c\s+`. 2. These regexes only match commands where `-c` immediately follows the executable name. 3. This allows an attacker to bypass the blocklist by inserting other flags (e.g., `python3 -P -c '...'`), leading to OS command injection.
cves.root_cause: The application failed to properly sanitize shell arguments, allowing attackers to bypass blocklists using argument permutation (specifically involving rm flags in Android).
cves.vulnerable_pattern: Permitting arbitrary flag permutation or insufficient filtering of command arguments that allows escaping intended blocklists.

The fix commit directly addresses these patterns by replacing them with more robust regexes that account for intervening flags, proving that the original implementation in the blamed commit was the source of the insufficient filtering.
screening_verification.vuln_description: The application failed to properly sanitize shell arguments, allowing attackers to bypass blocklists using argument permutation (specifically involving rm flags in Android).

These regexes only match commands where `-c` immediately follows the executable name. 3. This allows an attacker to bypass the blocklist by inserting other flags (e.g., `python3 -P -c '...'`), leading to OS command injection.

The fix directly replaces these strings, so this commit is the origin of that argument-injection bypass vector.

screening_verification.reasoning: The blamed commit introduced the `is_allowed` logic that relies on a simple blocklist and a naive tokenization approach (`split_whitespace().next()`). This implementation is what the fix commit identifies as vulnerable, specifically the lack of protection against argument permutation (like `python -Bc`) and shell glob characters (like `pass[w]d`) used to bypass the filters.
screening_verification.vuln_description: The application failed to properly sanitize shell arguments, allowing attackers to bypass blocklists using argument permutation (specifically involving rm flags in Android).
screening_verification.vulnerable_pattern: Permitting arbitrary flag permutation or insufficient filtering of command arguments that allows escaping intended blocklists.
screening_verification.causal_chain: 1. The blamed commit adds the `ShellSecurityConfig::is_allowed` method. 2. It introduces the initial regex-based blocklist (e.g., `python[23]?\s+-c\s+`) which was too restrictive. 3. It establishes the logic of checking only the first token or literal matches without accounting for shell globbing or complex argument permutations, creating the bypass vector.

agent_verdicts.reasoning: This commit introduced the allowlist feature and the vulnerable logic `if self.allowlist_mode != Off && !self.allowlist.is_empty()` plus a first-token-only check using `split_whitespace().next()`. That design lets a command like `git status; <second command>` pass strict allowlisting if the first token is allowlisted, and it makes an empty strict allowlist silently behave like Off. The screening hypothesis was directionally right that this commit introduced the allowlist flaw, but wrong to attribute the python/perl/ruby/node regexes to it; those came later in `d3480ca...`.

cves.description:
A flaw was found in mirror-registry where an authenticated user can trick the system into accessing unintended internal or restricted systems by providing malicious web addresses. 

When the application processes these addresses, it automatically follows redirects without verifying the final destination, allowing attackers to route requests to systems they should not have access to.
cves.how_introduced: The blamed commit implemented the HarborAdapter, which utilized requests (or similar) without explicitly disabling redirects or validating the final URL destination. This created the vulnerable state that allowed for subsequent SSRF exploitation.
cves.root_cause: The application allowed Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF) because it followed redirects when processing user-provided web addresses without validating the final destination against allowed targets.
cves.vulnerable_pattern: Fetching remote URLs using library functions (like requests) without checking destination IP/host allowlists or disabling automatic redirect following

cves.description: OpenClaw ACP client has permission auto-approval bypass via untrusted tool metadata
cves.how_introduced: The blamed commit implemented `resolveToolNameForPermission`, which retrieves tool names from metadata (`_meta`) and raw input (`rawInput`) using `readFirstStringValue`. Because this implementation lacked length constraints and strict regex validation, it allowed malicious tool names to pass through and be processed by the auto-approval logic.
cves.root_cause: The OpenClaw ACP client failed to properly validate tool metadata during permission auto-approval, allowing malicious actors to bypass authorization checks by manipulating untrusted metadata fields.
cves.vulnerable_pattern: Implicitly trusting tool metadata fields to authorize actions without server-side verification or strict schema enforcement.

cves.description: zeptoclaw has Android device shell blocklist bypass via argument permutation
cves.how_introduced: This commit created `src/tools/android/actions.rs` and introduced `device_shell()`. In that initial implementation, commands were normalized and checked with `lower.contains()` against literal substrings including `"rm -rf"` and `"rm -r"`, which only catches exact spellings and misses argument permutations like `rm -fr`, `rm -r -f`, `rm --recursive --force`, and path-prefixed/busybox forms. The fix replaces that exact logic with `rm_invocation_args()`/`is_rm_recursive_force()` token-and-flag parsing, showing that the flawed literal-substring design was the bug. The same commit also exposed the reachable `"shell"` Android action in `src/tools/android/mod.rs`, so it both introduced and enabled the vulnerable path.

cves.description: Fickling has Static Analysis Bypass via Incomplete Dangerous Module Blocklist
cves.how_introduced: This commit introduced the `StackGlobal.run()` validation `if not module.isidentifier() or not attr.isidentifier(): raise ValueError(...)`. That rejects dotted module names such as `multiprocessing.util`, which are exactly the kind of imports the 2026 fix adjusted to `all(m.isidentifier() for m in module.split("."))`. Because `check_safety()` does not wrap analysis in exception handling, this validation made dotted dangerous imports evade or break static analysis, so it is a real introducer for the dotted-module bypass fixed by `eb299b45`.

cves.description: The openml/openml.org web application version v2.0.20241110 uses incremental user IDs and insufficient email ownership verification during email update workflows. An authenticated attacker controlling a user account with a lower user ID can update their email address to that of another user with a higher user ID without proper verification. This results in the victim's email being reassigned to the attacker's account, causing the victim to be locked out immediately and unable to log in. The vuln
cves.how_introduced: This commit ("Bypass Flask") introduced the vulnerability into the Next.js application (version v2.0). It ported the flawed "update before verify" logic into `app-next/src/app/api/user/profile/route.ts`, allowing users to change their email address directly in the database without prior confirmation. Furthermore, it introduced the vulnerable login query in `app-next/src/app/api/auth/[...nextauth]/route.ts` (`SELECT ... FROM users WHERE email = ?`), which returns the first matching record (lowest ID). Since the database lacks a UNIQUE constraint on email, this combination allows an attacker with a lower user ID to shadow a victim's account by updating their email to match the victim's, causing the lockout described in the CVE.

cves.description: AutoGPT has SSRF vulnerability in ReadRSSFeedBlock
cves.how_introduced: This commit rewrote `parse_feed` to use `urllib.request.urlopen(url, timeout=30)` and added only a basic `http/https` scheme check plus size limits. The later SSRF fix (`a6a2f7145892...`) replaces this exact code with `Requests(...).get(url)`, which uses `validate_url` to block private/blocked IP ranges and is documented as preventing SSRF. Because this commit reimplemented the vulnerable fetch path without SSRF defenses, it is also a bug-introducing commit under the reimplementation rule.

cves.description:
HTTP::Session2 versions before 1.12 for Perl for Perl may generate weak session ids using the rand() function.

The HTTP::Session2 session id generator returns a SHA-1 hash seeded with the built-in rand function, the epoch time, and the PID. The PID will come from a small set of numbers, and the epoch time may be guessed, if it is not leaked from the HTTP Date header. The built-in rand() function is unsuitable for cryptographic usage.

HTTP::Session2 after version 1.02 will attempt to use the /d
cves.how_introduced: This repository-root commit already contains lib/HTTP/Session2/Random.pm with the exact vulnerable fallback that 9cfde4d removes: `substr(Digest::SHA::sha1_hex(rand() . $$ . {} . Time::HiRes::time()),int(rand(4)),31);` when /dev/urandom is unavailable. Blame of the pre-fix Random.pm lines 9-35 points to ^f8a08a8, and ServerStore's generate_session_id call sites also blame to ^f8a08a8, showing the weak generator and its use were present from this commit. The later fix commit replaces that design with Crypt::SysRandom-based generation.

cves.description: HomeBox Missing User Authorization
cves.how_introduced: This commit replaced the older token-based GET flow with direct file serving from `GET /v1/items/{id}/attachments/{attachment_id}`. The handler started calling `ctrl.svc.Items.AttachmentPath(r.Context(), attachmentID)` and then `http.ServeFile` with no group/item authorization on the attachment, which is exactly the GET-path flaw fixed by passing `ctx.GID` in e159dd8a. Even if earlier attachment access was already weak, this commit introduced the direct vulnerable download path present at the fix site.

cves.description: OpenClaw: Pairing setup codes exposed long-lived shared gateway credentials instead of short-lived bootstrap tokens
cves.how_introduced: This commit introduced the iOS QR/deeplink pairing path in `apps/shared/OpenClawKit/Sources/OpenClawKit/DeepLinks.swift`, adding `GatewayConnectDeepLink` with `token` and `password` fields and `fromSetupCode()` parsing of `{url, token?, password?}` payloads. The same commit also added QR output to `extensions/device-pair/index.ts`, so the vulnerable long-lived credentials were both rendered into QR setup codes and consumed by the iOS flow. The fix later changes this exact path to use `bootstrapToken` instead.
